headline: East Malaysian Indigenous Rights Under MA63 Still Not Codified
context: 57 years after MA63, indigenous peoples of Sabah and Sarawak — Iban, Kadazan-Dusun, Bidayuh, and others — still lack a comprehensive federal framework recognising their distinct rights.

cards.big: MA63 promised to protect Sabah and Sarawak's indigenous communities.
cards.sub: 57 years later, native customary rights are still governed by colonial-era ordinances that predate the agreement.

cards.big: The Sarawak Land Code 1958 and Sabah Land Ordinance 1930 govern native rights.
cards.sub: Both were drafted by colonial administrators. Neither has been comprehensively updated for constitutional alignment.

cards.big: 1.8 million hectares of NCR land claims are pending in Sarawak alone.
cards.sub: Average court resolution time is 12 years. 450 cases are active in the Sarawak High Court.

cards.big: New Zealand's Treaty of Waitangi Tribunal has resolved 2,500 indigenous claims since 1975.
cards.sub: Malaysia has no equivalent tribunal. Each claim must be individually litigated.

cards.big: Colonial-era land laws governing post-independence indigenous rights is not heritage. It is neglect.
A dedicated native rights tribunal for Sabah and Sarawak — modelled on New Zealand or Australia — would resolve claims faster, cheaper, and more justly than the current court-by-court approach.
